result.markdown:
---
title: "Deep Dive Expression: compiling ncRNA machinery fastas"
author: "Kathleen Durkin"
date: "2026-04-13"
categories: ["E5-coral"]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
execute: 
  eval: TRUE
engine: knitr
---


In my last post I used GLM 5.1 (via OpenWork) to compile a list of protein machinery important to miRNA and lncRNA biogenesis and function. I've synthesized this to an Excel sheet of proteins and associated gene names, `ncRNA_machinery.xlsx`. It and all other files associated with this post are available at `./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas`

Now I need protein fastas for all of these, preferably from a close relative of our coral species. To get these, I'll be using the [Uniprot protein database](https://www.uniprot.org/) with the following searching approach:

Use search parameters to limit results to Cnidarians, and to exclude fragmentary/incomplete sequences. I also restricted to proteins that were identified based on evidence at protein level, evidence at transcript level, or based on homology -- this will *exclude* proteins with a predicted or uncertain identification basis.

> (taxonomy_id:6073) AND (fragment:false) NOT (existence:4) NOT (existence:5)

I then included search terms for each protein/gene, generally in the format:

> ((protein_name:PROT_ABBRV) OR (gene:GENE_ABBRV))

This was occasionally modified to also include a search for full protein or gene names (for example, "Exportin-5", in addition to "XPO5"). This was sometimes necessary because many sequences are not annotated with the "standard" gene name.

I then downloaded sequence results in `.fasta` and `.fasta.gz` file formats. 

Some of the selected proteins weren't annotated in any cnidarians, so I instead searched in bilaterians and restricted to only reviewed entries. These are highlighted in the `ncRNA_machinery.xlsx` file.

I realized after almost fully completing my protein list that I'd been too permissive in my searches and, in some cases, proteins had "snuck through" because their names indicated a functional association with the searched protein. For example, when searching for Hsp90 proteins, entries for "Hsp90 chaperone protein kinase-targeting subunit" also showed up and were downloaded. 

To address this, I again turned to GLM 5.1. I gave it both the protein .xlsx file and the set of fastas/fasta.gz files. I instructed check all fasta headers and identify any instances of unintended proteins.

Prompt:

> At "/Users/Owner/.local/share/opencode/tool-output/tool_d89c3f7bd001SdDIyZGD890bfd" is a temp file that lists fasta and fasta.gz file names in the first column and Uniprot fasta headers in the second column. At "/Users/Owner/Downloads/The\ Big\ One/Documents/UW/Roberts\ Lab/OpenWork/ncRNA_machinery.xlsx" there’s a spreadsheet that contains in the first and second columns proteins and corresponding gene names for protein machinery related to ncRNA biogenisis and function.

> When compiling fastas for these proteins, some related but not targeted proteins slipped through because of overly permissive searches. Get a list of all targeted proteins from the xlsx file. Then search the file listing fasta headers and check for any fasta headers corresponding to proteins that are not actually included in the xlsx. If you find such instances, output both the fasta header and its source fasta/fasta.gz file.



Unzip all .fasta.gz files, then combine all fastas


::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
# Unzip all .gz files
gunzip ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/*.fasta.gz

# Combine all fasta files into one
cat ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/*.fasta > ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

grep -c '^>' ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta

grep '^>' ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta | head -3
```


::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
1027
>tr|A0A0C2N1X6|A0A0C2N1X6_THEKT Nuclear cap-binding protein subunit 1 OS=Thelohanellus kitauei OX=669202 GN=RF11_00163 PE=3 SV=1
>tr|A0A2B4SX82|A0A2B4SX82_STYPI Nuclear cap-binding protein subunit 1 OS=Stylophora pistillata OX=50429 GN=ncbp1 PE=3 SV=1
>tr|A0A6P8J329|A0A6P8J329_ACTTE Nuclear cap-binding protein subunit 1 OS=Actinia tenebrosa OX=6105 GN=LOC116306115 PE=3 SV=1
```


:::
:::


From the "interlopers" file compiled agentically, extract all the accession numbers I want to exclude from my compiled fasta. I'm expecting 152 accessions, plus the extra 4 from a second-round search.


::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

grep "Acc:" interlopers.txt | awk '{print $NF}' > interloper_accessions.txt

# Append the extra 4 from a second-round search at the bottom
awk -F'\t' '/^[0-9]+\t[A-Z0-9]+\t/ {print $2}' interlopers.txt >> interloper_accessions.txt
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
wc -l ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/interloper_accessions.txt
```


::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     156 ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/interloper_accessions.txt
```


:::
:::


Nice! So I have the 156 proteins I want to exclude from my compiled fasta


::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

# filter out sequences with interloper accessions
awk 'NR==FNR {acc[$1]; next} 
     /^>/ {skip=0; for(a in acc) if(index($0, a)) {skip=1; break}} 
     !skip' interloper_accessions.txt ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta > ncRNA_machinery_filtered.fasta
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

echo "Before: $(grep -c '^>' ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta)"
echo "After:  $(grep -c '^>' ncRNA_machinery_filtered.fasta)"
```


::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Before: 1027
After:  871
```


:::
:::


Perfect! The filtered file contains 156 fewer sequences than the original, just as expected!

Finally, most of the protein sequences have non-standard gene names as a consequence of using unreviewed Trembl entries from cnidarians (e.g., using source-specific names for transcripts, like "LOC###"). We'll probably want standardized gene names down the like, since full protein names can be lengthy. As far as I'm aware, there's no good way to look up protein names and obtain corresponding gene names in a standardized way, so I think I have to fall back on manual curation. I used Claude Opus 4.6 to help me with this task of parsing protein names to obtain standardized gene names, and obtained an R script for this purpose: `standardize_gene_names.R`


::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

Rscript standardize_gene_names.R ncRNA_machinery_filtered.fasta ncRNA_machinery.fasta
```

Check

::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

grep -c "^>" ncRNA_machinery.fasta
grep "^>" ncRNA_machinery.fasta | head -10
```


::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
871
>tr|A0A0C2N1X6|A0A0C2N1X6_THEKT Nuclear cap-binding protein subunit 1 CGN=NCBP1 OS=Thelohanellus kitauei OX=669202 GN=RF11_00163 PE=3 SV=1

>tr|A0ABM4DB04|A0ABM4DB04_HYDVU Nuclear cap-binding protein subunit 1-like CGN=NCBP1 OS=Hydra vulgaris OX=6087 GN=LOC136089457 PE=3 SV=1
```

:::
:::


Great! The manually-corrected fasta has the same number of sequences, the original gene names (GN:) were not overwritten, and each sequence now contains a curated gene name field (CGN:) with standardized gene names.

So I now have a final fasta file containing protein sequences for ncRNA protein machinery! The majority of sequences were obtained from cnidarians, but a small subset weren't annotated in any cnidarians and were instead sourced from reviewed bilaterian entries. 

I'll now use this fasta, in combination with the epimachinery fasta provided by Hollie and Steven to look for epigenetic protein machinery in the miRNA targets of our DDE data (and probably in the timeseries data in the future)
